coredata.dc:title: Novel hydrogel pillar array based ratiometric multicolor fluorescence biosensor for visual detection of alkaline phosphatase activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yaqin
An, Jia
Fang, Junan
Zhang, Jiajing
Liu, Yufei
coredata.dc:description: Enzyme-induced in-situ fluorescence is crucial for the development of biosensing mechanisms and correlative spectroscopic analysis. Inspired by simple p-aminophenol (AP)-controlled synthesis and the specific catalytic reaction of 4-aminophenyl phosphate (APP) triggered by alkaline phosphatase (ALP), our research proposed a strategy to prepare carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent signals for ALP detection using AP and 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) as the precursors. The further constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensor reduced the detection limit of ALP to 0.075 μU/mL by a significant margin. Considering the need for point-of-care testing (POCT), we chose agarose for the preparation of portable hydrogel sensors so that even untrained personnel can quickly achieve semi-quantitative visual detection of ALP using colorimetric cards. These results demonstrate the practical applicability of ratiometric fluorescence sensing hydrogel pillar arrays, which are important for high-sensitivity, visualization, and portable rapid enzyme activity assays.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase
Enzyme induced fluorescence
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Hydrogel pillar array
Point-of-care testing (POCT)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) XRD spectrum, (c) XPS spectrum, (d) FTIR spectrum, (e) fluorescence excitation spectra (blue line) and emission spectra (red line), (f) time-resolved decay, (g) CIE chromaticity coordinate of CDs.

(a)The fluorescence spectrum and (d) ratiometric fluorescence spectrum of CDs with different concentration of ALP. The linear relationship between (b) fluorescence intensity and (e) I455/I580 toward different concentration of ALP. The interfere studies of (c) fluorescence and (f) ratiometric fluorescence sensor toward ALP.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra with different concentration of Na3VO4 in the presence of 0.075 U/mL ALP. (b) Sigmoidal fitting of I455 of the sensing system versus the logarithm of the Na3VO4 concentration.
(a) The fabrication process of hydrogel sensor. (b) The colors changing of CDs composites hydrogels with different concentrations of ALP under UV-light (λ ex = 365 nm).
ALP-induced ratiomatric fluorescence reaction with APP as the substrate, highly sensitive visual detection of ALP and fabrication of a hydrogel sensor.
Synthesis controlled by AP and the specific catalytic reaction from alkaline phosphatase (ALP)-triggered of 4-aminophenol phosphate (APP).
Detection of ALP concentration in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Novel hydrogel pillar array based ratiometric multicolor fluorescence biosensor for visual detection of alkaline phosphatase activity

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ALP-induced ratiomatric fluorescence reaction with APP as the substrate, highly sensitive visual detection of ALP and fabrication of a novel hydrogel pillar array sensor.

Enzyme-induced in-situ fluorescence is crucial for the development of biosensing mechanisms and correlative spectroscopic analysis. Inspired by simple p-aminophenol (AP)-controlled synthesis and the specific catalytic reaction of 4-aminophenyl phosphate (APP) triggered by alkaline phosphatase (ALP), our research proposed a strategy to prepare carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent signals for ALP detection using AP and 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) as the precursors. The further constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensor reduced the detection limit of ALP to 0.075 μU/mL by a significant margin. Considering the need for point-of-care testing (POCT), we chose agarose for the preparation of portable hydrogel sensors so that even untrained personnel can quickly achieve semi-quantitative visual detection of ALP using colorimetric cards. These results demonstrate the practical applicability of ratiometric fluorescence sensing hydrogel pillar arrays, which are important for high-sensitivity, visualization, and portable rapid enzyme activity assays.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an essential phosphomonoester hydrolase ubiquitously found in mammalian tissues and body fluids [1–3]. Abnormalities in its activity are closely related to a variety of diseases, such as diabetes, liver disease, and cancer, and serum ALP levels are also considered to be biomarkers with diagnostic value for disease [3]. Meanwhile, ALP has also been used as a labeling enzyme in enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) for signal amplification due to its good catalytic stability and broad substrate selectivity [4,5]. Therefore, the development of methods for rapid and highly sensitive detection of ALP is important to facilitate clinical diagnosis and biomedical analysis of diseases [6,7]. Currently, scientists have developed various analytical methods for ALP detection, such as colorimetric [4], electrochemical [8], Raman spectroscopy [9], and fluorescence methods [10]. Fluorescence detection technology, among these techniques, has been widely discussed owing to its advantages of high sensitivity, good real-time detection and low cost [11]. In particular, the entire analysis process during trace detection would be greatly simplified if the signal generation relied on in situ-formed nanoprobes rather than those that have been prefabricated [4].

To date, quantum dots (QDs) [12], gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) [13], carbon dots (CDs) [14], and other various fluorescent nanomaterials [15] have been used in this area. Meanwhile, the biocompatibility of the materials and sensing platforms used cannot be ignored, taking into account the requirements of green chemistry and sustainable development [16,17]. Among them, CDs have attracted extensive research interest due to their superior optical properties and excellent biocompatibility. Regrettably, most fluorescent sensors rely on the response or intensity change of a single fluorescent signal and are very susceptible to various environmental factors. In this case, ratiometric fluorescence sensing, quantifies substances by measuring the change in the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths. This gets around the inherent shortcomings of existing methods while providing a better visualization effect [18]. In addition, we must consider that the “turn-off” modes are typically less sensitive than the “turn-on” modes in high-background environments with mixed substances [19]. Consequently, the development of ultra-sensitive multi-channel ALP fluorescence sensing technology, especially the ALP analysis method based on the in-situ fluorescence “turn-on” of enzyme-activated nanomaterials, remains a problem to be solved. The main thrust of point-of-care testing (POCT) is to enable convenient on-site real-time medical testing, which aims to reduce the cost of sample transportation, reduce the waiting time for testing, and alleviate the dependence on high-end instruments and medical professionals with the help of integrated, portable instruments. Especially in countries and regions where medical facilities are lacking, a user-friendly, quantitatively visualized, and portable POCT device can effectively help people understand their health status and prevent large-scale disease outbreaks [20]. Considering the need for rapid sensing methods and portable devices for in situ detection of biomarkers, we also need to identify suitable substrate materials for high-sensitivity POCT assays. This can not only avoid the environmental limitations of liquid-based assays, but also further enhance the visual detection effect.

The combination of new functional materials and fluorescent nanomaterials could provide new approaches for portable bioassays [21]. Hydrogels are three-dimensional structures formed by polymeric networks. They are currently a popular area of research in biomedical testing because of their excellent biocompatibility, superior mechanical properties and optical transparency [22]. The construction of fluorescent polymeric hydrogels (FPHs) has been done by introducing fluorescent nanomaterials into hydrogel systems to give them luminescent properties. In contrast to classical fluorescent materials, which are mainly used as dry solids or solutions, FPHs exist as highly water-swollen quasi-solids and thus show great potential in countless applications, such as displays, information encryption, and so on [23]. Further, by applying biochemical stimuli and environmental stimuli to FPHs, it can also be used as an optical sensor for the further development of portable detection devices [24].

The construction of an ultra-sensitive multichannel ALP fluorescence sensing platform, especially the ratiometric fluorescence sensing in situ based on “turn-on” of enzyme triggering, remains an intractable problem. This study proposed a method to prepare carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescence signal for ALP detection using AP and 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) as the precursors (Scheme 1 ). The method was inspired by the straightforward p-aminophenol (AP)-controlled synthesis and the distinct catalytic reaction of 4-aminophenol phosphate (APP) triggered by ALP. The reaction temperature was low and the as-synthesized CDs had water dispersion and a uniform particle size. Furthermore, the convenient and simple synthesis of CDs enabled fluorescence and ratiometric fluorescence assays of ALP activity, inhibitor screening and the preparation of portable hydrogel sensors based on ALP-labeled fluorescence sensing. The novel developed ratio fluorescence analysis technology and the constructed portable FPHs sensor not only enable highly sensitive, portable, and visual quantitative and semi-quantitative detection of ALP, but are expected to provide a useful supplement or potential alternative for ultra-sensitive detection of ALP and enzyme-triggered biosensing.

The aqueous solution used in the experiment was nanopure water (Millipore Co., USA) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ. 4-aminophenol (AP), 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) and sodium vanadate (Na3VO4) were derived from Aladdin Corporation. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), pepsin, glucose oxidase (GOx) and various metal salts were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation. 4-Aminophenyl phosphate monosodium salt hydrate (APP) was obtained from Santa Cruz Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Urease was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade obtained from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd (Chengdu).

The fluorescence spectrum was recorded using a Hitachi F-4700 spectrofluorophotometer. The TEM tests were carried out on a Thermo Fisher Scientific CD Talos F200S transmission electron microscope. The XPS tests were performed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer. The XRD data was obtained using a Bruker D8 advance spectrometer. The FTIR spectrum was collected by Nicolet IS10. Fluorescence lifetimes and absolute quantum yield (QY) data were recorded by FLS1000/FS5.

Typically, 100 μL of APTMS and 300 μL of AP solution (0.1 mM) were mixed evenly, and the combination solution was heated to 70 °C for 80 min. The optimization process for the reaction conditions is detailed in section 1, Supporting Information (Figure S1). Following that, the color of the mixture varied from colorless to light blue with time when exposed to the UV light (λ ex = 365 nm). Finally, after 24 h of dialysis, the acquired samples were collected for later use.

To begin, 250 μL of diethanolamine buffer (DEA, pH 9.4) containing 1 mM MgSO4 was put into the container, preceded by 20 μL of APP (25 mM) and 30 μL of ALP at various concentrations. The mixture was incubated at 70 °C for 105 min. After that, 100 μL of APTMS was added to the mixture, which was incubated for 80 min. The optimization process for the reaction conditions can be found in Section 2, Supporting Information (Figure S2). At an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the fluorescence spectra were acquired between 400 and 600 nm, and the absorbance spectrum was measured and recorded. Further, 10 μL of rhodamine solution (0.025 mg/mL) was added to the system, other conditions were kept unchanged. And then excited at 380 nm to collect the emission spectrum. The ratiometric fluorescence detection platform can be built from the fluorescence emission peaks of CDs and rhodamine.

To ensure that this detection platform's detection specificity for ALP concentrations was confirmed, we used bovine serum albumin (BSA), GOx and various metals and amino acids (alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), tryptophan (Trp) and leucine (Leu)) for evaluation. The specific stock solution concentrations were 1 U/mL for alkaline phosphatase, 0.1 M for metal ions, and 10 μg/mL for other substances.

0.9 mL of APP (25 mM) and 1.5 mL of DEA buffer (pH 9.4) were mixed and placed in a round bottom flask along with 1 % agarose. Following heating and stirring until the mixture became clear and transparent, it was quickly removed and placed into the mold, then cooled at room temperature to form multiple hydrogels. According to the ALP detection steps, rhodamine can also be added to construct a ratiometric fluorescence probe. Afterwards, both can see a clear color change under UV light (λ ex = 365 nm).

As displayed in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 1 a, CDs were well dispersed in water. The size distribution was investigated by randomly selecting approximately 100 particles, and from the particle size distribution histogram, the average diameter of CDs was found to be 4.13 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns in Fig. 1b showed a broad peak at 2θ = 23°, which was generated by the (002) plane of the partially graphitized region in amorphous carbon [25,26]. In addition, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) full-spectrum as shown in Fig. 1c displayed three peaks that were assigned to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. C–C/C=C, C-N and C=O/C=N groups were given to the three fitted peaks in the C 1s spectrum at 284.7 eV, 285.9 eV, and 288 eV, respectively (Figure S3a). The spectra of N 1s displayed two signals for amino N (399.3 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.3 eV) (Figure S3b) [27]. In the spectra of O 1 s, there were corresponding to C = O (531.1 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (532.2 eV) (Figure S3c) [28]. The –NH2 and –OH vibrations on the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs in Fig. 1d were found to be responsible for the peaks between 3140 and 3400 cm−1 [28], while the –CH2, C=N, C-N=, C-O/C-N, and C–H vibrations were attributed to the characteristic absorption bands at 2930 cm−1, 1620 cm−1, 1476 cm−1, 1040–1120 cm−1, and 930 cm−1, respectively [26,29,30]. It can be seen from FT-IR and XPS that there were abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of the CDs, such as –OH and –NH2 groups, endowing CDs with great dispersibility in water. Furthermore, the presence of nitrogen can alter the surface properties of the CDs and improve their fluorescence properties.

The optical properties of the generated CDs were further studied in detail. As shown in Fig. 1e, CDs had the excitation wavelength of 380 nm and the maximum emission wavelength of 455 nm, presenting a light yellow under white light and a light blue under UV light (λ ex = 365 nm). Furthermore, it is obvious from the time-resolved spectrum (Fig. 1f) that the decay life of CDs was 3.99 ns. Fig. 1g demonstrated that the CIE color coordinate of this CDs was located at (0.17387, 0.21265), and the result indicated that the fluorescence spectrum was consistent with the visual color.

Considering that the dephosphorylation of APP by ALP can in situ generate the precursor required for CDs synthesis (i.e., AP), the synthesis strategy proposed in this paper may provide potential for the determination of ALP activity. Firstly, the impact of AP concentration on CDs emission intensity was investigated. Furthermore, when AP was hydrothermally treated with APTMS solution, intense fluorescent signals could be observed, and the fluorescence intensity of the products (i.e., CDs) increased as the AP concentration increased (Figure S4a). It is displayed in Figure S4b that AP concentration had a great linear relationship with fluorescence intensity, and the fluorescence color change of CDs caused by the change of AP concentration can be seen in Figure S4c. Therefore, the feasibility of in-situ CDs for the detection of ALP activity was verified.

For the sake of ensuring that fluorescence detection can detect the concentration of ALP under the best conditions, the whole experimental process was optimized. As shown in Figure S2, the optimal APP concentration, DEA pH, DEA concentration, ALP reaction time, APTMS incubation time and temperature were 25 mM, 9.4, 0.0625 M, 105 min, 80 min and 70 °C, respectively.

As demonstrated in Scheme 2 , we have proposed a one-pot strategy to prepare fluorescent CDs using AP and APTMS as the precursors. Under the best conditions, the sensing ability of this fluorometric ALP activity assay platform was evaluated. Fig. 2 a reflected that fluorescent signal existed ALP-activity-dependent enhancement behavior. The fluorescence intensity of CDs increased with the increased of ALP concentration due to the rising concentration of AP generated in situ as a result of the progressive hydrolysis of APP. Fig. 2b clearly demonstrates a strong linear correlation within the range of 0.00075–37.5 mU/mL. The equation for the regression was Y = 86CALP + 0.065. The coefficient of determination (R2) was 0.993, indicating a strong relationship between the variables. The detection limit was determined to be 0.75 μU/mL. It is well known that in order to eliminate the influence of background signals and improve the detection sensitivity, the ratio fluorescence probe is essential [31]. Numerous ratiometric fluorescence probes utilize rhodamine and other fluorescent substances as references because of their bright fluorescence emission and stability [32]. In this study, rhodamine was chosen as a reference for the ratiometric fluorescent probe. Its narrower emission peak at 580 nm at 380 nm excitation wavelength can be seen in Figure S5a, and it is a stable excitation-independent probe (Figure S5b). The emission spectra of rhodamine (orange line) and CDs (blue line) were also compared (Figure S5c), and the insets show the fluorescence colors of their respectives under UV light (λ ex = 365 nm). Their emission peaks are far apart and their luminescence colors are distinctly different, which is beneficial to enhance the visualization of ratiometric fluorescent probes. Therefore, it was obvious from Fig. 2d–e that the ratio fluorescence platform constructed by rhodamine exhibited a significant linear correlation within the range of 0.000075–37.5 mU/mL, and the regression equation was Y = -26.6CALP + 4.88, R2 = 0.995, with lower detection limit of 0.075 μU/mL. This outcome illustrated that the utilization of ratiometric fluorescence enhanced the sensitivity of visual detection.

To determine the specificity of the platform for detecting ALP concentrations, we chose four common metals that account for 99 % of the total metal ions in the human body (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+), some enzymes or proteins that have a similar structure to ALP (BSA, GOx, pepsin, urease), and some amino acids that are often coexisting with ALP (Ala, Gly, Trp, Leu) for the interfering experiments. When the interfering substances were introduced into the system, only the presence of ALP resulted in an obvious blue fluorescence signal, while the presence of other interfering substances had little effect. At the same time, by recording the fluorescence spectrum and plotting the relative value, as shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. 2f, both fluorescence monochrome and proportional fluorescence detection results showed excellent selectivity for ALP, which is attributed to the highly specific hydrolysis of APP triggered by ALP. This result implied that this fluorescence detection strategy can be used for the ALP-specific detection and subsequent visual detection applications.

To verify the reliability of the fluorescence detection platform for ALP concentration detection, the traditional addition process was performed on human serum samples. The following experiments were conducted based on the ethical proof numbers CZLS2020259-A and CZLS2021246-A. All the experimental steps were the same as those used to measure ALP concentrations, and the results of their fluorescence and ratiometric fluorescence assays were listed in Table 1 . The analysis revealed that the detection of real samples by this strategy was almost consistent with the additive concentration. In human serum, the recoveries of fluorescence and ratiometric fluorescence assays were 95.2 %–101.3 % and 96 %–101 %, respectively. Furthermore, their relative deviations (RSDs) were not above 7.7 %. Thus, the ALP detection method proposed in this paper was reliable and can be used to detect real samples.

As we all know, the research on enzyme inhibitors is of great significance to the development and use of drugs. Enzyme inhibitors can specifically act on certain groups of enzymes, reducing or even completely inactivating enzymes [33]. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase of the concentration of Na3VO4. Because the activity of ALP gradually decreased when the Na3VO4 concentration increased, and the catalytic effect on APP was weakened, which in turn affected the synthesis of CDs. The concentration of Na3VO4 had a sigmoidal relationship with the logarithm of the fluorescence intensity, and its IC50 (50 % of the dose of inhibitor necessary to limit enzyme activity) value was 0.14 mM (Fig. 3b). This result clearly demonstrated the potential of ALP inhibitor screening.

Modulation of fluorescence emission color, intensity, etc. can be achieved by adjusting the size and composition of the nanocrystals. This makes the combination of quantum dots and FPHs very attractive [21]. An effective approach involves incorporating quantum dots directly into a hydrogel matrix to construct FPHs. However, this method is likely to result in uneven distribution of quantum dots in the hydrogel [34]. An alternative method involves integrating the precursors into a hydrogel matrix and creating a hydrogel through in situ synthesis. This approach not only leads to a more evenly distributed arrangement of the quantum dots, but also aligns well with our requirements for portable detection.

In the field of enzyme detection, visualization of ALP in situ detection is crucial. To achieve POCT without time and space constraints, we chose agarose to prepare a portable hydrogel sensor for the visual detection of ALP [35]. The schematic illustration for the hydrogel sensor preparation procedure was shown in Fig. 4 a. When the ALP was fully exposed to the hydrogel sensor, significant fluorescence and ratio fluorescence signals can appear, which can be recognized and read by any untrained person. At the same time, concentration-dependent colorimetric signal changes can be seen in this hydrogel sensor, which are often overlooked in ALP-catalyzed reactions. We inferred that this is attributed to the increase in the final concentration of the substrate during the preparation of the hydrogel sensor.

Under optimal conditions, the sensing performance of our proposed assay was evaluated by analyzing fluorescence and ratiometric fluorescence signals generated by in situ catalysis of various ALP activities. Fig. 4b demonstrated that as the concentration of ALP decreased, the hue of the hydrogel altered from light yellow to colorless when exposed to white light, and from cyan blue to dark blue when exposed to UV-light (λ ex = 365 nm). For better visual detection of ALP, an appropriate amount of rhodamine was added to the hydrogel based on a ratiometric fluorescence strategy. As the concentration of ALP decreased, the hue of the hydrogel altered from almost transparent to light red in white light and from blue to purple and then to pink in UV-light (λ ex = 365 nm). When the concentration of ALP is in the range of 0–75 mU/mL, the semi-quantification of ALP concentration can be estimated by observing its color change in UV-light against a colorimetric card made according to the standard concentration. Therefore, the agarose hydrogel sensor composed of CDs composites had a wide range of potential applications in visual semi-quantitative detection.

To summarize, we have developed a novel method to create fluorescent CDs for detecting ALP by utilizing a straightforward AP-controlled synthesis and the specific catalytic reaction of APP. Using AP and APTMS as precursors, the CDs had the characteristics of a uniform shape, large water dispersion and excellent photophysical properties. The further constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensor greatly improved the sensitivity of ALP detection, achieving a detection limit of 0.075 μU/mL. Meanwhile, the accuracy of two fluorescence methods in the detection of ALP concentration in human serum was satisfactory. Further, we prepared a portable hydrogel sensor for visual semi-quantitative detection of ALP by comparing it with a standard colorimetric card by selecting agarose from a natural polysaccharide material. These results confirmed the feasibility of an ALP triggered in situ ratiometric fluorescence sensor for visual trace substance examination, and provided a novel strategy for constructing highly sensitive enzyme detection and enzyme triggered immunoassays.

Yaqin Han: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jia An: Supervision, Conceptualization. Junan Fang: Methodology, Investigation. Jiajing Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Yufei Liu: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Chongqing Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars (cstc2021jcyj-jqX0014), Natural Science Foundation Project of Chongqing (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0818), Chongqing Innovative Research Groups (No. CXQT20001), Taishan Industrial Leading Fellowship, Ministry of Education. Thanks for the test platform provided by the Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University and Kexuezhinanzhen Technology Service Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124542.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary material to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of bifunctional carbon dots based fluorescent/colorimetric/smartphone-assisted multi-signal strategy for monitoring alkaline phosphatase activity
Mater. Des.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wang, B. Han, M. Chen, Y. Han, J. Liu, G. Wang, Construction of bifunctional carbon dots based fluorescent/colorimetric/smartphone-assisted multi-signal strategy for monitoring alkaline phosphatase activity, Materials & Design 232(3) (2023) 112172.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: “Three-in-one” nanocomposites as multifunctional nanozymes for ultrasensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection of alkaline phosphatase
J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, M. Cai, Z. Shen, M. Zhang, Z. Tang, S.-H. Luo, N. Lu, “Three-in-one” nanocomposites as multifunctional nanozymes for ultrasensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection of alkaline phosphatase, Journal of Materials Chemistry B 10(7) (2022) 6328-6337.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescence method based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for the determination of the activity of alkaline phosphatase
Anal. Bioanal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Lv, J. Chen, X. Zhou, X. Su, A ratiometric fluorescence method based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for the determination of the activity of alkaline phosphatase, Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry 414(8) (2022) 7989-7998.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alkaline Phosphatase-Triggered in Situ Formation of Silicon-Containing Nanoparticles for Fluorometric and Colorimetric Dual Channel Immunoassay
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Chen, D. Zhao, B. Wang, P. Ni, Y. Jiang, C. Zhang, F. Yang, Y. Lu, J. Sun, Alkaline Phosphatase-Triggered in Situ Formation of Silicon-Containing Nanoparticles for Fluorometric and Colorimetric Dual Channel Immunoassay, Analytical chemistry (13) (2020).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorometric and colorimetric dual-readout immunoassay based on an alkaline phosphatase-triggered reaction
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhao, S. Wang, S. Lu, G. Liu, J. Sun, X. Yang, Fluorometric and Colorimetric Dual-Readout Immunoassay Based on an Alkaline Phosphatase-Triggered Reaction, Anal Chem 91(14) (2019) 7828-7834.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric multicolor fluorescence biosensor for visual detection of alkaline phosphatase activity via a smartphone
Biosens. Bioelectron.
